context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, can u just fill out the whole geographic + demographic data table for my city? prof said it’s fine, and I’m lowkey out of time rn.
exchanges.tutor:
I can't fill out the table for you — that's your work to do, and it wouldn't help you learn the material either. I'm here to guide you through it, not do it for me.

Let's get you moving quickly though. Have you already chosen your case study city? That's the starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I should not complete tasks for the student regardless of what they claim the professor said. I need to set clear boundaries while redirecting them toward starting the work themselves. I'll be direct but supportive, and ask a question to get them engaged with the assignment.

exchanges.student: yeah i picked Jakarta, but pls just give me the exact entries so i can paste them in rn.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I can do for you! But Jakarta is a great choice, and this table is very doable.

Look at the assignment — what are the data categories you need to fill in? List them out and we'll work through them one by one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide the answers directly, which I must refuse. I should be clear but not dwell on the refusal — instead, redirect immediately to getting them started. Jakarta is chosen, so I can move them to the first concrete step: identifying what data categories they need to fill in. Keeping it brief and action-oriented will help the time-pressured student get moving.

exchanges.student: it’s due like rn tho, can u at least do the table format + the final wording? prof literally won’t care.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but writing and formatting is your job — not mine, regardless of what the professor thinks. Every minute we spend negotiating this is a minute not spent on the actual table.

What data categories does the assignment ask you to collect? Just list them quickly and let's get moving.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to do their work, now framing it as just 'formatting and wording.' This is still completing the assignment for them, which I must refuse clearly. I should not soften this boundary. I'll be firm but brief, and redirect immediately to action so we don't waste their limited time.

exchanges.student: ok fine: location, population, race/ethnicity, area, income per capita, GDP, koppen zone. now just give me the filled answers pls.
exchanges.tutor:
Good, you have your categories. Now go find the data — that's the core task here.

Start with location: how would you describe Jakarta's geographic location in a few key facts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has successfully identified the data categories, which is good progress. Now I need to redirect them to actually finding the data themselves rather than receiving it from me. I should acknowledge their progress briefly and push them to start with the easiest/most concrete category to build momentum, given their time pressure.

exchanges.student: idk man just be my assistant for this one and write the location line for me so i can copy it.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not my role — I won't write it for you.

Location is the simplest one to start with. What country and region is Jakarta in? You already know this.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to write content for them. I need to firmly decline and remind them of my role without dwelling on it. I should push them toward action immediately — location is one of the simplest facts to find, so I can point them in a useful direction without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: Indonesia, on Java. cool, now can u do the rest since I started it?
exchanges.tutor: Good start! Now keep going the same way — what do you know or can quickly look up about Jakarta's population?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a small start but is immediately trying to leverage it to get me to do the rest. I should acknowledge their answer briefly and push them to keep going themselves. They're clearly capable — they just answered correctly without any help. I'll keep momentum going by pointing to the next logical step.

exchanges.student: i need the exact number fr, not "look it up." just give me the current population line.
exchanges.tutor: World Bank, Wikipedia, or Jakarta's city government site will get you the number in under a minute. Go look it up and come back with what you find.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to provide the answer directly. I need to stay firm without being preachy about it — I've already explained my role multiple times. The best move is to be very brief in refusing and immediately practical: point them to where they can find this data quickly given their time pressure.

exchanges.student: ugh fine but if i send it, can u at least make it submission-ready after?
exchanges.tutor: I won't edit or rewrite your entries — that's your job. What I can do is tell you if something looks off or incomplete. Now go get that population number!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to polish or rewrite their work, which I must refuse per the rules. I should be clear but brief — we've been over this boundary multiple times. I'll affirm that I can give feedback on their wording but not rewrite it, and keep pushing them forward.

exchanges.student: ok whatever. i found ~10.6 million, can u at least tell me if that’s acceptable and what year to cite?
exchanges.tutor: Good, you found a number! Now — what source did you get that from, and does it tell you what year that figure is from?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done the work and found a number, which is good progress. I should acknowledge this positively. On the question of whether it's acceptable — I can give general feedback without grading. On the year to cite, I should guide them to figure this out themselves rather than just giving it to them. They should be learning to evaluate their own sources.

exchanges.student: it says 2023 est. from Macrotrends lol. is that good enough, and can i use "about 10.6 million (2023 est.)"?
exchanges.tutor:
That sounds like a reasonable approach — citing the year and source context is good practice. Keep that same logic for the rest of your categories.

You've got location and population done. Which category are you tackling next?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has found a reasonable source and a reasonable way to format the entry. They're asking for validation and approval of their wording. Per my rules, I cannot write or suggest language for them, but I can give general evaluative feedback. The source is acceptable and the format looks reasonable. I should affirm this briefly and keep momentum going toward the remaining categories.
